title: Notebook 3 - Measurement of Wage Outcomes for a Cohort
text:
4 Introduction
Welcome to the third notebook of Module 2, where will expand our focus to a framework that facilitates longitudinal investigations, a cohort analysis. In doing so, we will reintroduce a fact table initially discussed in the second notebook that tracks individuals at the program level by their spells, or episodes of service. Here, we will develop a cohort analysis to help address our same focused research topic introduced in the previous notebook, which is aimed at identifying participants who had filed a UI claim in a specified quarter, then following them into their future workforce outcomes.
Previously, we applied a cross-sectional analysis to the TANF and WIOA data, which allowed us to better understand the volume of individuals enrolled in multiple programs at a specific moment in time. Since cross-sections are restricted to particular snapshots, and do not account for shocks though, they are limited in providing a framework for tracking experiences over time. Contrast this approach to that of a cohort analysis.
In creating a cohort, we will denote a reference point where each member of our cohort experienced a common event - this could be entry into a program, exit from a program, or any other shared experience across a set of observations. With this setup, we can better understand and compare the experiences of those encountering the same policies and economic shocks at the same time, especially across different subgroups, or even cohorts.

5 Introduction
Welcome to the
This notebook covers the details of the data model tables we have constructed for the training.
Analyses involving administrative data often require: - Linking observations from multiple sources - Mediating differences in semantics - Mediating differences in grain (month versus quarter) - Mediating differences in cardinality and the potential to unintentionally exclude or overreport values - Facilitating intuitive and efficient processing and analysis of very large record sets - Mediating differences in names and relationships over time
This notebook will introduce and demonstrate some helpful techniques for linking administrative data while mediating the above issues. The output of the notebook should provide a example, logic based framework that meets the needs of many projects and can be easily customized to include additional variables or characteristics.
The linked data asset documented in this notebook has already been completely created and loaded in the tr_ihep_2025_pa database as tables beginning with an “PA” prefix, with the final fact table titled PA_FACT_Quarterly_Observation_new. This notebook will not create or load duplicative copies of the linked dataset but rather cover the techniques used to construct and load the model and hopefully serve as a resource to use when building future linked data sets.

title: Understanding the Data Model Tables
text:
Primary data generation
Unlike reference data that is consistent across states (CIP, NAICS for industry codes), primary data refer to the unique collection of persons, employers, or households served by each state. A state can have many different references to the same real-world entity, and the process followed here is the processing of assembling a set that has one member (record) for each unique instance of an entity in the real world.
This primary record can merge attributes from multiple sources, resulting in a golden record with a higher completeness than is available in individual sources. When multiple references to the same entity have different values, those differences are resolved through a process called survivorship in which decisions are made about which value to keep (most recent, most frequent, highest quality source, etc.).
In our data, we already have a primary person table developed as part of this training (conveniently titled pa_mdim_person) that contains any individuals from any data source in the PASSHE Data System. The table already includes some time invariant variables, such as race/ethnicity and gender, date of birth and Pell grant status, and contains one record per person. Due to our intention of applying the PVF to this data, we will leverage some data from this table, as well as some other time invariant variables. Since the PVF often assigns cohorts to indviduals by their highest, most recent degree, we will add this information to our time-invariant, person table. Additionally, we will limit our person table to those who appear in the enrollments data.

-- person mastering
with ssn_key as (
    select ssn, dense_rank() over (order by ssn) as person_key
    from (
        select distinct ssn
        from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
    ) a
),

gender_tab as (
    select ssn, sex_code, count(*) as cnt,
           row_number() over (partition by ssn order by count(*) desc,
                              case when sex_code = 'F' then 1
                                   when sex_code = 'M' then 2
                                   else 3 end) as rn
    from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment e2
    where sex_code is not null
    group by ssn, sex_code
),

gender_tab2 as (
    select * 
    from gender_tab
    where rn = 1
),

eth_counts as (
  select
    ssn,
    race_ethnicity,
    count(*) as cnt
  from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
  where race_ethnicity is not null
  group by ssn, race_ethnicity
),
eth_ranked as (
  select
    ssn,
    race_ethnicity,
    row_number() over (
      partition by ssn
      order by 
        case 
          when lower(race_ethnicity) in ('unknown', 'missing', 'not reported', '') then 2
          else 1
        end,
        cnt desc
    ) as rn
  from eth_counts
),
eth as (
  select ssn, race_ethnicity
  from eth_ranked
  where rn = 1
)
,
dob_tab as (
    select e2.ssn, e2.birthday, count(*) as cnt,
           row_number() over (partition by ssn order by count(*) desc, birthday) as rn
    from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment e2
    where birthday > '1900-01-01'
    group by ssn, birthday
),

dob_tab2 as (
    select *
    from dob_tab
    where rn = 1
),

pell as (
    select 
        fa.ssn, 
        case when max(pell) > 0 then 1 else 0 end as pellrecipient
    from ds_pa_sshe.financial_aid fa
    group by fa.ssn
)
select
    --sk.person_key,
    cl.ssn,
    cl.birthday,
    c2.race_ethnicity,
    c3.sex_code,
    coalesce(p.pellrecipient, 0) as pellrecipient
   -- case when p.pellrecipient is null then 0 else p.pellrecipient end as pellstatus
from dob_tab2 cl
left join eth c2 on cl.ssn = c2.ssn
left join gender_tab2 c3 on cl.ssn = c3.ssn
--left join ssn_key sk on cl.ssn = sk.ssn
left join pell p on cl.ssn = p.ssn
group by 1,2,3,4,5;

title: Understanding the Data Model Tables
text:
Fact Tables
The fact table stores the actual observations (facts) of interest. Since this table often contains large numbers of records, it will ideally be comprised of a small number of bytes per row and primarily consist of indexed foreign keys to dimension tables and observation-specific measures. This allows for storage of large records sets with low storage cost and extremely high query performance (extremely helpful for supporting dashboards).
In this example, the fact table is at the grain of one row per person per quarter. We will create a record for every quarter between the first and last quarter_id values generated in the time dimension table, regardless of enrollment status in a given quarter. These missing observation quarters are materialized because non-enrollment may be just as interesting for some analyses and longitudinal analysis benefits from consistent representation across time periods of consistent grain.
Some of our cohort members have observations for multiple enrollments or completions in a single quarter (or term). Since our unit of analysis is the person, not the person-enrollment/completion combination, we need to resolve these one-to-many relationships into a single observation while retaining the information pertinent to analysis. In this example, the primary enrollment and completion per quarter, along with the associated information, were identified and recorded based on the institution with the most amount of credit bearing hours or highest degree, respectively. In order to minimize loss of potentially relevant information, the total enrollments and completions is also included on each observation.
We also matched financial aid information on the primary institution of enrollment - recall that financial aid is recorded yearly, so you may encounter repeat values, and be careful about summing them up within a given financial aid academic year. In our example, there may be multiple records for an individual in a year, even when matching on institution, so the record with the highest financial aid information was selected for the surviving financial aid values. The optimal survivorship strategy may vary based on the intended audience and purpose.

This query is looking at large volumes of data and will run for several minutes if not filtered for specific observations.


with id as (
    select distinct pmp.ssn 
    from tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_mdim_person pmp 
    limit 100 -- to shorten run time
),
--find all quarter_id values
q as (
    select distinct quarter_id
    from tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  prq 
),
--join them to get all combinations of ssn and quarter_id
tot as (
    select id.ssn, q.quarter_id 
    from id 
    cross join q 
),
--join quarter_id to the enrollments data
enr_quarter as (
    select e.*, q.Quarter_ID, q.academic_term , q.academic_year 
    from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment e
    join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  q 
    on q.acad_pd_code = e.academic_period_code::numeric
),
-- find enrollment status
status as (
        select distinct tot.ssn,
    tot.quarter_id,
    case when enr.ssn is null then 'N' else 'Y' end as Enrollment_Status --if they show up in the enrollment quarter in a given value of
    from tot 
    left join enr_quarter enr --left join to keep all values of ssn and quarter_id
    on tot.ssn = enr.ssn and tot.quarter_id = enr.quarter_id 
),
--find number of total enrollments per quarter
enroll_per_q as (
    select status.ssn, status.quarter_id, count(distinct(e.ipeds_unitid)) as num_enrollments
    from status 
    left join enr_quarter e on (e.ssn = status.ssn and e.quarter_id = status.quarter_id)
    group by status.ssn, status.quarter_id
),
rank_inst as (
    select ke.ssn, ke.ipeds_unitid, krq.Financial_Aid_Year,
            row_number() over (
            partition by ke.ssn, krq.Financial_Aid_Year order by ke.ssn, krq.Financial_Aid_Year, sum(ke.credits_attempted_for_term) desc
        ) as num 
    from ds_pa_sshe.enrollment ke 
    join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  krq 
    on krq.acad_pd_code = ke.academic_period_code::numeric 
    group by ke.ssn, ke.ipeds_unitid, krq.Financial_Aid_Year
),
dom_inst as (
    select ssn, ipeds_unitid, financial_aid_year 
    from rank_inst 
    where num = 1
),
dom_inst_info as (
    select dom_inst.*, krq2.quarter_id,
        ke2.credits_attempted_for_term , 
        ke2.credits_completed_for_term ,
        ke2.is_pa_resident_for_billing ,
        ke2.ug_gr ,
        ke2.fulltime_parttime ,
        ke2.is_first_time ,
        ke2.major1_cip6_code ,
        ke2.award_level ,
        row_number() over (
            partition by ke2.ssn, ke2.ipeds_unitid, krq2.quarter_id order by ke2.ssn, ke2.ipeds_unitid, krq2.Quarter_ID, ke2.credits_attempted_for_term  desc
        ) as num 
    from dom_inst 
    join ds_pa_sshe.enrollment ke2 on 
        (dom_inst.ipeds_unitid = ke2.ipeds_unitid and dom_inst.ssn = ke2.ssn)
    join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods krq2 on (krq2.Financial_Aid_Year = dom_inst.financial_aid_year and 
        krq2.acad_pd_code = ke2.academic_period_code::numeric )
        
),
dom_inst_info_ranked as (
select * 
from dom_inst_info 
where num = 1
),

--match to financial aid information, eventually join only on the primary institution information
fin_aid as (    
    select 
    distinct 
    kfa.ssn,
    kfa.academic_year , 
    max(kfa.total_gift_aid ) as totalgrantsallsources, --in case duplication on quarter_id and ipeds_unitid for grants with different values
    max(kfa.total_fed_loans) as totalloansallsources, --in case duplication on quarter_id and ipeds_unitid for loans with different values
    max(kfa.total_fed_workstudy) as totalworkallsources, --in case duplication on quarter_id and ipeds_unitid for work-study with different values
    max(kfa.pell) as pell_grants, --in case duplication on quarter_id and ipeds_unitid for pell grants with different values
    max(kfa.total_price) as totalprice,
    kfa.ipeds_unitid,
    krq.quarter_id
    from ds_pa_sshe.financial_aid kfa 
    join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods krq on krq.academic_year = substring(kfa.academic_year from 1 for 4)::numeric 
    group by ssn, kfa.academic_year, ipeds_unitid, quarter_id
),
comp_quarter as (
    select kd.*, q.Quarter_ID, q.academic_year, q.academic_term
    from ds_pa_sshe.completions kd--  ds_ky_kystats.kpeds_degree kd 
    join tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  q 
    on q.acad_pd_code = kd.academic_period_code 
    
),
--rank primary completion if multiple completions in a given quarter_id
comp_ranked as (
--if they show up in the completion quarter in a given value of quarter_id then they completed, otherwise not
    select tot.ssn, tot.quarter_id,
    case when comp.ssn is null then 'N' else 'Y' end as completion_status,
    comp.award_level, --  degreerank,
    --comp.kpeds_degreelevelshortdesc,
    comp.major1_cip6_code,
    comp.ipeds_unitid,
    row_number() over (
        partition by tot.ssn, tot.quarter_id order by tot.ssn, tot.quarter_id, comp.award_level
    ) as num 
    from tot 
    left join comp_quarter comp --left join to keep all values of ssn and quarter_id
    on tot.ssn = comp.ssn and tot.quarter_id = comp.quarter_id 
),
--identify primary completion per quarter_id
first_comp as (
    select ssn,
    quarter_id, 
    completion_status,
    --degreerank,
    award_level,
    major1_cip6_code,
    ipeds_unitid
    from comp_ranked 
    where num = 1
),
--find amount of completions per quarter_id
comp_per_q as (
    select ssn,
    quarter_id,
    case when completion_status = 'N' then 0 else max(num) end as num_completions
    from comp_ranked 
    group by ssn, quarter_id, completion_status
),
--THIS IS WHERE WE ADDED IN THE WAGE RECORDS CTES
Wage_Rank AS (
    SELECT
    P.ssn,
    Q.Quarter_ID,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY P.ssn, Q.Quarter_ID ORDER BY W.wages_real DESC) AS RANK,
    W.wages_real, --,
    W.employer_id ,
    w.naics_level6_code 
    FROM 
    ds_pa_dli.wage_records W
    JOIN tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_mdim_person P ON (W.ssn = P.ssn)
    JOIN tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_dim_quarterly_academic_periods  Q ON ((W.yr = Q.Calendar_Year) AND (W.qtr = Q.Calendar_Quarter))
),
Primary_Employer_Wage AS (
    SELECT
    WR.ssn,
    WR.Quarter_ID,
    WR.wages_real AS Primary_Employer_Wages,
    WR.employer_id as Primary_Employer_ID, 
    wr.naics_level6_code
    FROM
    Wage_Rank WR
    WHERE
    WR.RANK=1
),
All_Employer_Wage AS (
    SELECT 
    WR.ssn,
    WR.Quarter_ID,
    COUNT(WR.employer_id) AS Employer_Count,
    SUM(WR.wages_real) AS Total_Wages
    FROM Wage_Rank WR
    GROUP BY
    WR.ssn,
    WR.Quarter_ID
)
select 
tot.ssn, 
tot.quarter_id, 
status.enrollment_status,
d.ipeds_unitid as enrolled_primary_institution,
d.credits_attempted_for_term as enrolled_primary_hours_attempted,-- kpeds_creditbearinghours ,
d.credits_completed_for_term as enrolled_primary_hours_completed,
d.fulltime_parttime as enrolled_primary_full_time_ind,
d.is_first_time as enrolled_first_time,
d.major1_cip6_code as enrolled_primary_major,
d.award_level as enrolled_primary_degree_sought,
d.is_pa_resident_for_billing as enrolled_primary_residency,
enroll_per_q.num_enrollments as number_enrollments,
fin_aid.totalprice as primary_institution_total_price,
fin_aid.totalgrantsallsources as primary_institution_grant_aid,
fin_aid.totalloansallsources as primary_institution_loan_aid,
fin_aid.totalworkallsources as primary_institution_work_aid,
fin_aid.pell_grants as primary_institution_pell,
first_comp.completion_status as completion_status,
first_comp.award_level as completed_primary_degree,
 substring(first_comp.major1_cip6_code from 1 for 6) as completed_primary_major,
first_comp.ipeds_unitid as completed_primary_institution,
comp_per_q.num_completions as number_completions,
CASE WHEN PEW.ssn IS NULL THEN 'N' ELSE 'Y' END AS Employed,
PEW.Primary_Employer_ID,
PEW.Primary_Employer_Wages,
PEW.naics_level6_code,
AEW.Total_Wages,
AEW.Employer_Count
from tot 
join status on (tot.ssn = status.ssn and tot.quarter_id = status.quarter_id)
left join dom_inst_info_ranked d on (tot.ssn = d.ssn and tot.quarter_id = d.quarter_id)
join enroll_per_q on (tot.ssn = enroll_per_q.ssn and tot.quarter_id = enroll_per_q.quarter_id)
--join on primary enrolled institution for financial aid info, left join to preserve all values of ssn and quarter_id
left join fin_aid on (tot.ssn = fin_aid.ssn and tot.quarter_id = fin_aid.quarter_id and d.ipeds_unitid = fin_aid.ipeds_unitid)
join first_comp on (tot.ssn = first_comp.ssn and tot.quarter_id = first_comp.quarter_id)
join comp_per_q on (tot.ssn = comp_per_q.ssn and tot.quarter_id = comp_per_q.quarter_id)
LEFT JOIN Primary_Employer_Wage PEW ON (PEW.ssn=tot.ssn) AND (PEW.Quarter_ID=tot.Quarter_ID) 
LEFT JOIN All_Employer_Wage AEW ON (AEW.ssn=tot.ssn) AND (AEW.Quarter_ID=tot.Quarter_ID) 
order by tot.ssn, tot.quarter_id


Cost fact table
A similar logic is used to create our second fact table, which measures the total cost of attendance, measured for each person, institution, and award level, including tuition, fees, room and board.
Because some students are not present in the financial aid table, we impute the cost of attendance using the tuition_and_fees_lookup table for a given year, institution and residency status.

WITH student_first_year AS (
  SELECT
    ssn,
    MIN(SUBSTRING(academic_year, 1, 4)::NUMERIC) AS first_year
  FROM ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
  WHERE is_first_time = 'Y'
  GROUP BY ssn
),

-- Step 2: Max transfer credits per student
max_transfer_credits AS (
  SELECT
    ssn,
    MAX(credits_transferred) AS max_credits_transferred
  FROM ds_pa_sshe.enrollment
  WHERE is_transfer = 'Y'
  GROUP BY ssn
),

-- Step 3: Base data with net cost, credit totals, and transfer flag
base_data AS (
  SELECT
    e.ssn,
    e.ipeds_unitid,
    e.ug_gr,
    e.is_pa_resident_for_billing,
    e.is_transfer,
    COUNT(1) AS n,
       SUM(e.credits_completed_for_term) AS credits, -- total credits in year
  avg(fa.total_price) - avg(fa.total_gift_aid) as net_cost ,
  avg(fa.tuition)- avg(fa.total_gift_aid ) as net_cost_noroom,
  avg(fa.total_fed_loans) as total_loans,

    -- Flag for actual zero net cost
    CASE
      WHEN SUM(fa.total_price) - SUM(fa.total_gift_aid) <= 0 THEN TRUE
      ELSE FALSE
    END AS is_actual_zero_net_cost,

    SUBSTRING(e.academic_year, 1, 4)::NUMERIC AS yr,

    -- Yearly sum, preserving NULL if any part is NULL
    (SUM(fa.total_price) - SUM(fa.total_gift_aid)) / NULLIF(COUNT(1), 0) AS yearly_sum

  FROM ds_pa_sshe.enrollment e
  LEFT JOIN ds_pa_sshe.financial_aid fa
    ON e.ssn = fa.ssn
   AND e.academic_year = fa.academic_year
   AND e.ug_gr = fa.ug_gr
  WHERE SUBSTRING(e.academic_year, 1, 4)::NUMERIC >= 2009
  GROUP BY
    e.ssn, e.ipeds_unitid, e.ug_gr, e.is_pa_resident_for_billing, e.is_transfer, SUBSTRING(e.academic_year, 1, 4)
),

-- Step 4: Add first-year indicator and max transfer credits
enriched_data AS (
  SELECT
    b.*,
    mt.max_credits_transferred,
    CASE WHEN b.yr = f.first_year THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE END AS is_first_year
  FROM base_data b
  LEFT JOIN student_first_year f ON b.ssn = f.ssn
  LEFT JOIN max_transfer_credits mt ON b.ssn = mt.ssn
),

-- Step 5: Add cost estimates from lookup table, and compute total_cost
enriched_with_sumcost AS (
  SELECT 
    enriched_data.*,

    -- Lookup-based imputation for cost
    CASE 
      WHEN credits <= 24 THEN 
        COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0) * COALESCE(credits, 0) 
        + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_parttime, 0)
      ELSE 
        COALESCE(tafl.tuition_fulltime, 0) 
        + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_fulltime, 0) 
        + COALESCE(tafl.room, 0) 
        + COALESCE(tafl.board, 0)
    END AS sumcost,
    -- Transfer tuition cost only in first year using max credits
    CASE 
      WHEN is_transfer = 'Y'  THEN COALESCE(max_credits_transferred, 0) * COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0)
      ELSE 0
    END AS transfer_tuition_cost,

    -- Total cost = sumcost + one-time transfer credit cost in first year if applicable
    (CASE 
        WHEN credits <= 24 THEN 
          COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0) * COALESCE(credits, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_parttime, 0)
        ELSE 
          COALESCE(tafl.tuition_fulltime, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_fulltime, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.room, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.board, 0)
     END
     + CASE 
         WHEN is_transfer = 'Y'  THEN COALESCE(max_credits_transferred, 0) * COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0)
         ELSE 0
       END) AS total_cost,
 (CASE 
        WHEN credits <= 30 THEN 
          COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0) * COALESCE(credits, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_parttime, 0)
        ELSE 
          COALESCE(tafl.tuition_fulltime, 0) 
          + COALESCE(tafl.fees_required_fulltime, 0) 
     END
     + CASE 
         WHEN is_transfer = 'Y' AND is_first_year THEN COALESCE(max_credits_transferred, 0) * COALESCE(tafl.tuition_per_credit, 0)
         ELSE 0
       END) AS total_cost_noroom
  FROM enriched_data
  LEFT JOIN ds_pa_sshe.tuition_and_fees_lookup tafl
    ON enriched_data.ipeds_unitid = tafl.ipeds_unitid
   AND enriched_data.yr = tafl.ipeds_year
   AND enriched_data.ug_gr = tafl.ug_gr
   AND enriched_data.is_pa_resident_for_billing = tafl.is_pa_resident_for_billing
),

-- Step 6: Replace yearly_sum with imputed value if missing
final_with_adjusted_sum AS (
  SELECT 
    *,
    CASE 
      WHEN yearly_sum = 0 OR yearly_sum IS NULL THEN sumcost
      ELSE yearly_sum
    END AS adjusted_yearly_sum
  FROM enriched_with_sumcost
),

-- Step 7: Compute cumulative cost
final_with_running_sum AS (
  SELECT 
    *,
    SUM(total_cost) OVER (
      PARTITION BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr, is_pa_resident_for_billing
      ORDER BY yr
      ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS updated_running_sum,
    SUM(total_loans) OVER (
      PARTITION BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr, is_pa_resident_for_billing
      ORDER BY yr
      ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS updated_running_loans,
    SUM(total_cost_noroom) OVER (
      PARTITION BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr, is_pa_resident_for_billing
      ORDER BY yr
      ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS updated_running_sum_noroom
  FROM final_with_adjusted_sum
),

-- Final Output: One row per student-institution-program with max cost
ranked_rows as (SELECT *
FROM (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr
           ORDER BY updated_running_sum DESC
         ) AS rn
  FROM final_with_running_sum
) ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr)
SELECT
  ssn,
  ipeds_unitid,
  ug_gr,
  is_pa_resident_for_billing,
  is_transfer,
  transfer_tuition_cost,
  case when updated_running_sum <0 then 0 else updated_running_sum end AS total_cost, --floor total cost at 0
  case when updated_running_sum_noroom <0 then 0 else updated_running_sum_noroom end AS total_cost_noroom,
  updated_running_loans as total_loans,
  yr AS last_year_included,
  --is_first_year,
  is_actual_zero_net_cost
FROM ranked_rows
ORDER BY ssn, ipeds_unitid, ug_gr;



Creating your own cohort
Since we performed the cohort filtering in R, we can save the final cohort definition as a csv file or a table in the tr_ihep_2025_pa schema. We can also save the file as a permanent table in R, which will allow us to leverage SQL’s join functionality to link the cohort to large tables that are unable to be read into R due to memory constraints. The following code blocks demonstrate how to convert an R data frame to a permanent table by creating the table nb_cohort_2015 in the VDC training workspace database tr_ihep_2025_pa from the data model table.

Note: Saving your team’s cohort as a permanent table is highly recommended. When doing so, please save your table with the prefix team# (ex. team1_cohort) to differentiate between tables for each project.


drop table if exists tr_ihep_2025_pa.nb_cohort_2015;
create table tr_ihep_2025_pa.nb_cohort_2015 as 
SELECT q01.ssn, q01.quarter_id as nb_quarter, 
q01.enrolled_primary_institution, 
q01.enrolled_primary_major_level,
q01.enrolled_primary_major

FROM (
  SELECT
    pa_fact_quarterly_observation_new.*,
    MIN(quarter_id) OVER (PARTITION BY ssn) AS min_qtr
  FROM tr_ihep_2025_pa.pa_fact_quarterly_observation_new
  WHERE (enrolled_primary_major_level = 'UG' AND enrollment_status = 'Y')
) q01
WHERE (quarter_id = min_qtr) AND (quarter_id = 60.0); -- modify your quarter id  to match the time you want

grant SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE, INSERT ON TABLE tr_ihep_2025_pa.nb_cohort_2015  to  group db_t00137_rw;

title: Notebook 4 - Applied Regression Analysis
text:
6 Introduction
Welcome to the fourth notebook of Module 2, where we introduce applied regression analysis. In this lesson, we will build on the data sets and analytic frameworks developed in previous notebooks to explore how regression models can help us quantify relationships between program participation and outcomes of interest. We’ll begin with linear regression, which allows us to estimate how continuous outcomes-such as quarterly wages-vary across individual characteristics and participation patterns. We’ll then extend these ideas to logistic regression, which is used when outcomes are categorical, such as whether an individual is employed or not.
In this notebook, we will reintroduce our familiar fact table at the individual-program level and use it to construct models that explain variation in outcomes based on demographic factors, prior experience, and participation indicators. These models allow us to move beyond descriptive comparisons and begin estimating the magnitude and direction of relationships, providing a foundation for more advanced causal analyses in future lessons.
Previously, our focus was on cohort analysis-tracking groups of individuals over time to compare their outcomes following a common reference point, such as program entry or exit. Regression analysis builds on this by allowing us to formally control for multiple factors simultaneously, isolating the unique association between each predictor and the outcome. In doing so, we can better understand how different characteristics and experiences contribute to observed differences in employment and wage outcomes.
By the end of this notebook, you will be able to specify and interpret both linear and logistic regression models using program data, assess model fit, and understand how these tools can be applied to policy and evaluation contexts. This provides an essential analytic bridge between descriptive analyses and more rigorous impact evaluation methods, such as propensity score matching and causal modeling.

The Purpose of these Notebooks

title: Notebook 4 - Applied Regression Analysis
text:
Final data cleaning
In this dplyr step, we recode some variables so they are more easily compared, and do some modification of variables so that numeric variables are all numeric.
We categorize the education variable into logical categorical levels, and we also do this for age as well.
We create the variable wp_enroll, which is 1 for anyone who participated in Wagner-Peyser or any combination of Wagner-Peyser and some other program.
Finally, to make interpretation of our regression model easier, we z-score the prior wage variables, so that a 1 unit change in these corresponds to a 1 standard deviation change in wages, instead of a change in 1 dollar.

reg_data <- reg_data |> 
  select(person_key, gender, education, age, eth_recode, co_enrolled, ui_quarterly_wages_prior_quarter,
         yearly_sum_prior, quarters_active_prior_2yr,ui_quarterly_wages ) |>
    mutate(across(c(quarters_active_prior_2yr , yearly_sum_prior, ui_quarterly_wages_prior_quarter), ~ replace_na(as.numeric(.x), 0))) |>
  filter(gender != 'Unknown',
         as.numeric(age ) >= 16) |> 
  mutate(edu_cat = case_when(education <12 ~ 'No HS',
                             education == 12 ~ 'HS/GED',
                             education %in% c(13:15) ~ 'Assoc/Some College',
                             education == 16 ~ 'Bachelors',
                             education %in% c(17:19) ~ 'Graduate degree',
                            TRUE  ~ NA_character_ ),
         age_n = as.numeric(age), 
         age_cat = case_when(age_n <25 ~ '16-25',
                             age_n >=25 & age_n <35 ~ '25-34',
                             age_n >=35 & age_n <45 ~ '35-44',
                             age_n >=45 & age_n <55 ~ '45-54',
                             age_n >=55 & age_n <65 ~ '55-64',
                             age_n >=65 ~ '65+',
                             TRUE  ~ NA_character_),
         wp_enroll = if_else(grepl("wp", co_enrolled, ignore.case = T), 1, 0),
         scale_prior_wages  = scale(ui_quarterly_wages_prior_quarter),
         scale_prior_year_wage = scale(as.numeric(yearly_sum_prior) ) ,
         quarters_active_prior_2yr = ifelse(is.na(as.numeric(quarters_active_prior_2yr)) ==T, 0,as.numeric(quarters_active_prior_2yr))) |>
  select(person_key, age_n, gender, eth_recode, edu_cat, age_cat, wp_enroll, scale_prior_wages, scale_prior_year_wage,quarters_active_prior_2yr , ui_quarterly_wages) 


head(reg_data, n = 50)


DBI::dbWriteTable(con,DBI::Id(schema = "tr_state_impact_ada_training", table = "nb_analysis_frame"),
  reg_data,                             # your data.frame
  overwrite = TRUE,               # or append = TRUE
  row.names = FALSE )

title: Notebook 4 - Applied Regression Analysis
text:
Summary of the model
The summary() function will summarize the results of the regression model.
When applied to a fitted regression model in R, the summary() function provides a detailed statistical summary of the model’s results.
For linear models (created with lm()), summary() displays:
Coefficients: The estimated effect of each predictor on the outcome, including the intercept.
Standard errors, t-values, and p-values: Used to assess whether each coefficient is statistically different from zero.
Residual statistics: Measures of how well the model fits the data, including the residual standard error and degrees of freedom.
R-squared and Adjusted R-squared: Indicators of how much variation in the outcome is explained by the model.
F-statistic: A test of the overall significance of the regression model.
In short, summary() translates the fitted model into interpretable numerical results, allowing you to evaluate both the size and the significance of each predictor’s effect.

summary(linear_model)

In this situation, the coefficient for Wagner-Peyser enrollment is negative, while theoretically it should be positive. This may suggest that we have selectivity with respect to program participation. We will revisit this later.
We also see significant effects of prior quarter wages and number of quarters worked in the previous 2 years.

title: Notebook 4 - Applied Regression Analysis
text:
Generating adjusted means
The emmeans (Estimated Marginal Means) package in R provides a powerful framework for interpreting fitted models, such as linear or logistic regressions. When we fit a model with multiple predictors, the raw coefficients can be difficult to interpret-especially when they involve categorical variables or interactions. The emmeans package helps translate those model results into meaningful, interpretable summaries.
When emmeans() produces its estimates, it is essentially doing three things:

Reconstructing predicted values from the model.
After a model is fit (e.g., using lm() or glm()), emmeans uses the estimated regression coefficients to calculate the expected value of the outcome for specified levels of one or more predictors. These are sometimes called model-based means or predicted margins.
Averaging (or “marginalizing”) over other variables in the model.
To isolate the effect of one predictor (e.g., wp_enroll), emmeans holds that variable at each of its levels (e.g., enrolled vs. not enrolled) while averaging over the observed or reference values of all other predictors in the model.

For categorical predictors, it averages across the levels, usually weighted by sample size (or equally if specified).

For continuous covariates, it typically uses the mean (or another reference value).
This produces adjusted means-the expected outcome at each category or condition, adjusted for other variables in the model.

Presenting the results on a meaningful scale.

For linear models, estimates are presented directly on the outcome scale (e.g., predicted quarterly wages).

For logistic regression and other generalized linear models, the underlying model is fit on the link scale (e.g., log-odds), but emmeans can transform results back to the response scale (e.g., probabilities).
Using type = "response" tells emmeans to report results in the metric that’s easiest to interpret-such as the predicted probability of employment or participation.




In Short
You can think of emmeans as a tool that answers questions like:

“What would the average wage be for men versus women, assuming all other variables stayed the same?”

It doesn’t change the model, it simply uses the fitted model to generate adjusted, interpretable summaries that reflect the relationships estimated by the regression.






You will need to install the emmeans package, using install.packages('emmeans')




library(emmeans) 

emmeans(linear_model, ~age_cat + gender + wp_enroll)

title: Notebook 4 - Applied Regression Analysis
text:
Graphing the adjusted means
This code first computes the estimated adjusted means for each combination of age_cat and gender using the fitted regression model linear_model. These means represent the predicted average wages, adjusted for other variables in the model.
The plot() function from emmeans automatically produces a base ggplot object showing these estimates and their confidence intervals. The second part of the code adds custom labels and a title using labs(), clarifying that the plot displays the model-adjusted wage averages by age and gender group.
In essence, this visualization helps interpret how expected wages differ across demographic subgroups, holding other model predictors constant.

mymeans <- data.frame(emmeans(linear_model, ~age_cat + gender+ wp_enroll))

mymeans |> 
  ggplot( aes(x = age_cat, y = emmean, color = gender)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower.CL, ymax = upper.CL), width = 0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ wp_enroll) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Estimated Wage",
    title = "Adjusted Means by W-P Enrollment Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

title: Notebook 4 - Applied Regression Analysis
text:
Summary of the model
For logistic or generalized linear models (created with glm()), summary() reports coefficient estimates, standard errors, and significance tests, but the coefficients are on the link scale (e.g., log-odds for logistic regression).

summary(logreg_model)

In this model summary, we see that there is a relatively linear trend with older individuals being less likely to participate in Wagner-Peyser, that there is no difference between men and women, and individuals with higher previous wages and with either a High school/GED degree, or High School not comlete, are more likely to participate in Wagner-Peyser.

title: Notebook 4 - Applied Regression Analysis
text:
Generating adjusted means
Here, we calculate the adjusted means of the probability of participating in the Wagner-Peyser program. To obtain probability estimates, we specify type = "response" to get the probability scale. If we do not do this, the estimates will be on the log-odds scale and will be harder to interpret.
To see the effect of the scaled wages, we can specify values to estimate the adjusted means at, here we specify values of 0 and 1, corresponding to values of average wages(0), and higher than average wages(1)

emmeans(logreg_model, ~age_cat +scale_prior_wages ,
        at = list("scale_prior_wages" = c(0, 1)),
         type = "response")

title: Notebook 4 - Applied Regression Analysis
text:
Graphing the adjusted means
In this graph, we see that the probability of enrollment in Wagner-Peyser is highest for younger individuals with higher than average wages. We also see the educational attainment differences, with those with a HS/GED or No High School, having higher probabilities of being in the program.

mymeans_log <- data.frame(emmeans(logreg_model, ~age_cat + edu_cat + scale_prior_wages,
                                  at = list("scale_prior_wages" = c(0, 1)),
                        type = "response"))

mymeans_log |> 
  ggplot( aes(x = age_cat, color = factor(scale_prior_wages), y = prob)) +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.5),size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = asymp.LCL, ymax = asymp.UCL), width = 0.1, position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
  facet_wrap(~ edu_cat) +
  labs(
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Probability of Enrollment",
    title = "Estimated Probability of Enrollment in Wagner-Peyser by Wage and Age"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

title: Notebook 5 - Propensity Score Matching
text: 7 Introduction

title: Notebook 5 - Propensity Score Matching
text:
Introduction to Propensity Score Analysis
Welcome to the fifth notebook of Module 2, where we introduce propensity score analysis as a method for estimating causal effects when random assignment is not possible. In this lesson, we will build on the regression techniques developed previously and explore how we can use propensity scores to construct comparison groups that are statistically similar to program participants. This approach helps reduce selection bias and isolate the estimated effect of program participation from pre-existing differences among individuals.
In this notebook, we will use the propensity score - the estimated probability of receiving treatment given a set of observed characteristics - to create a more balanced dataset for comparing treated and untreated individuals. By matching individuals with similar propensity scores, we can approximate the conditions of a randomized experiment and estimate how program participation influences outcomes such as wages or employment.
We will begin by estimating propensity scores using a logistic regression model based on characteristics such as age, sex, education and prior wages. We will then use these scores to perform nearest-neighbor matching, pairing each treated individual with one or more nonparticipants who have similar probabilities of treatment. Finally, we will assess the quality of the matches, examine covariate balance, and estimate the Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) to quantify the program’s impact.

Average treatment effect on the Treated (ATT)
The Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) represents the estimated average impact of a program or intervention on those individuals who actually participated. In other words, it measures how much better (or worse) the outcomes of participants are compared to what those same individuals would have experienced had they not received the treatment - assuming all relevant confounding factors have been accounted for.
Because we cannot directly observe what would have happened to participants in the absence of the program, the ATT is estimated by comparing each treated individual’s observed outcome to that of one or more matched nonparticipants with similar characteristics and similar propensity scores. By doing so, we approximate the counterfactual outcome - what the treated group’s outcomes likely would have been without the treatment.
In practice, the ATT tells us the average effect of treatment among those who received it, rather than in the entire population. This distinction is important: while the Average Treatment Effect (ATE) applies to everyone, the ATT focuses on the impact experienced by program participants themselves, which is often the most relevant measure for policy evaluation and program improvement.






NoteKey Concept



Propensity score analysis aims to reduce bias from observed differences between treated and untreated groups by matching individuals with similar probabilities of receiving treatment. This process mimics random assignment and improves causal interpretation in non-experimental settings.


Previously, our regression analysis allowed us to control for multiple factors in a single model to estimate adjusted associations between program participation and outcomes. Propensity score analysis builds on this foundation by focusing on balancing the data before estimating outcomes, making the treatment and comparison groups more directly comparable. This represents a key step toward more rigorous causal inference in program evaluation.






TipLearning Objectives



By the end of this notebook, you will be able to: - Estimate and interpret propensity scores using logistic regression. - Implement and assess matching methods using the MatchIt package. - Evaluate covariate balance and interpret the Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT). - Understand how propensity score methods relate to regression-based approaches and how they strengthen causal inference in observational data.

title: Notebook 5 - Propensity Score Matching
text:
Logistic regression model
This model mimics that of the previous notebook, where we do a logistic regression estimating the effects of the covariates on Wagner-Peyser enrollment.

logreg_model <- glm( wp_enroll ~ age_cat + gender + edu_cat + eth_recode+ scale_prior_wages + scale_prior_year_wage +scale(quarters_active_prior_2yr) ,
                     data = reg_data_cl,
                     family = binomial)

summary(logreg_model)







NonePerformance of the model





If you are interested in the accuracy of this logistic regression model, the caret package is useful for calculating various model fit criteria. First, we create the predicted values of the wp_enroll variable from the model itself, then create the confusion matrix for the model.
In this case, we see the model is not particularly accurate at predicting program participation, given what we have included in it.

reg_data_cl$pred_predict <- predict(logreg_model, type = "response")
reg_data_cl$pred_class <- ifelse(reg_data_cl$pred_predict >.5, 1, 0)

# install.packages('caret')

library(caret)

conf <- confusionMatrix(
  factor(reg_data_cl$pred_class),
  factor(reg_data_cl$wp_enroll),
  positive = "1"  # specify which class is considered "positive"
)

conf

title: Notebook 5 - Propensity Score Matching
text:
Creating the matched data set
Since this matching looks better, we will use it to create our dataset for additional analysis. The match.data() function will take the results and do this for us.
You can see in the matched dataset, a column named distance, which is the estimated propensity score for each case, and you can see the matched pair identifier in the subclass column, which shows which

matched <- match.data(m_nn2)


head(matched |> arrange(subclass), n = 50)

title: Notebook 5 - Propensity Score Matching
text:
Examining common support
We can also visualize the distribution of the propensity scores by the enrolled vs. non-enrolled people in the matched dataset, this allows us to examine the common support assumption when doing propensity score analysis.






Note



You will need to install the halfmoon packages prior to use, to do this type: install.packages('halfmoon')



library(halfmoon)
ggplot(
  matched,
  aes(distance, fill = factor(wp_enroll))
) +
  geom_mirror_histogram(bins = 50) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = abs) +
  labs(x = "propensity score", fill = "WP - enrolled")

In this case, it looks like the distribution of propensity scores in the treated and untreated groups have very similar distributions, which is what we are hoping for.

title: Notebook 5 - Propensity Score Matching
text:
Take-aways
This is not the only method to estimate propensity scores, and for those who are curious, consult the documentation for the MatchIt package, or you can type ?matchit in the R terminal for more information and options.

title: Notebook 6 - Exports
text:
8 Introduction
This notebook provides information on how to prepare research output for presentation while also ensuring disclosure guidelines are followed. It outlines how to prepare different kinds of outputs before submitting an export request and gives an overview of the information needed for disclosure review. Please read through the entire notebook because it will separately discuss all types of outputs that will be flagged in the disclosure review process.

title: Notebook 6 - Exports
text:
Export Guidelines

Each team is able to export up to 10 figures/tables
Every statistic for export must be based on at least 10 individuals (unique SSNs, not rows)

Statistics that are based on 0-9 individuals must be supressed

Counts must be rounded

Counts below 1000 must be rounded to the nearest ten
Counts greater than or equal to 1000 must be rounded to the nearest hundred
For example, a count of 868 would be rounded to 870, and a count of 1868 would be rounded to 1900.

Reported wages must be rounded to the nearest hundred
Reported averages must be rounded to the nearest tenth
Percentages and proportions must be rounded

The same rounding rules applied to counts must be applied to both the numerator and denominator before finding the percentage/proportion
Percentages must then be rounded to the nearest percent
Proportions must be rounded to the nearest hundredth

Exact percentiles cannot be exported

Instead, for example, you may calculate a fuzzy median, by averaging the true 45th and 55th percentiles

If you are calculating fuzzy wage percentiles, you will need to round to the nearest hundred after calculating the fuzzy median
If you are calculating fuzzy percentiles for counts of individuals, you will need to round to the nearest 10 if the count is less than 1000 and to the nearest hundred if the count is greater than or equal to 1000


Exact maxima and minima cannot be exported

Suppress maximum and minimum values in general
You may replace an exact maximum or minimum with a top-coded value or a fuzzy maximum or minimum value. For example: If the maximum value for earnings is 154,325, it could be top-coded as ‘100,000+’. Another permissible approach using this example would be calculating a fuzzy maximum value by using the formula below:

\[\frac{90th\ percentile\ of\ earnings + 154325}{2}\]
Complementary suppression

If your files include totals or are dependent on a preceding or subsequent file, you need to take into account complementary disclosure risks that is assessing if the figure totals or the separate figures, when read together, might disclose information about less then 10 individuals or employers in the data in a way that a single, simpler file would not. Team leads and export reviewers will work with you on implementing any necessary complementary suppression techniques

Regression Output

Output from regression analysis needs to be documented
Report the number of observations for each variable used in the analysis
For continuous variables, report the rounded number distinct SSN’s for complete observations
For Categorical variables, report the rounded number of distinct SSN’s for for each level of the categorical variable
Adjusted means must be rounded following suggestions above.

Help on Export Questions

Please consulte the ADRF user handbook for general guidelines on submitting exports:

title: Notebook 6 - Exports
text:
Supporting Documentation for Exports
You will need to provide a table with underlying counts of individual SSN’s for each statistic depicted in the file requested for export.

You will need to include both the rounded and the unrounded counts of individuals
If percentages or proportions are to be exported, you must report both the rounded and the unrounded counts of individuals for the numerator and denominator.

Code - Please provide the code written to create every output requested for export and the code generating every table (csv) with underlying counts. It is important for the ADRF staff to have the code to better understand what exactly was done and to replicate your results. Understanding how research results are created is important in understanding the research output. Thus, it is important to document every step of the analysis in your code file(s).

title: Notebook 6 - Exports
text:
Rounding Counts - Race
The first step is to construct the data frame we will display. Let’s start by calculating counts of the number of people in each racial category.

NOTE using n_distinct() is essential here to get the number of people, since each SSN/person key may be repeated in the data for each year they have wages.


race_counts<-analysis_data|> 
  group_by(eth_recode) |> 
  summarize(count=n_distinct(person_key))

race_counts

Remember, though, in order to follow the disclosure guidelines, we need to round our counts.

race_counts_rounded <- race_counts |>
  mutate(rounded_counts = if_else(as.numeric(count)<1000,
                                  round(as.numeric(count), digits=-1),
                                  round(as.numeric(count), digits=-2)))

race_counts_rounded

In order to export the above information, you will need to submit two tables:

race_counts_rounded - You will need to submit the table above under the export documentation section, in order to show the export reviewers both the unrounded counts as well as the rounded counts.
You will also need to submit an additional table under the export section. This table should remove the unrounded counts column. This is the table that will actually be exported from the ADRF, and the table you will receive when you export request is approved. The code chunk below will create a table that removes the raw count column from the race_counts_rounded table by utilizing the ! operator, which indicates we want to select all columns except for the count column.


race_counts_to_export <- race_counts_rounded |>
  select(!count)

race_counts_to_export

title: Notebook 6 - Exports
text:
Creating the bar chart
Now we can craft our bar chart, being sure to display the rounded counts. The only things we need to supply ggplot with to make this graph are the data frame to use, x and y values, and the geom_col() function so that it knows we want a bar plot.

race_counts_to_export |> 
ggplot( aes(x=eth_recode, y=rounded_counts)) +
geom_col()

As you can see, however, there are several things we could do to this graph to make it more presentation-ready.
First, let’s give the bars some color. We can do this by providing geom_col() with a fill argument listing our color of choice.
Next, we give the graph a title with ggtitle(). Then, using the theme() function, we center the title and rotate the x-axis tick labels 45 degrees.
Finally, we specify the x- and y-axis using more professional labels.

ggplot(race_counts_to_export,
    aes(x=fct_reorder(eth_recode, rounded_counts, .desc=T),
      y=rounded_counts)) +
  geom_col(fill='blue') +
  ggtitle("Analytic Cohort by Race & Ethnicity") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust=0.9),
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)) +
  ylab("# of People") +
  xlab("Race/Ethnicity") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Notebook 6 - Exports
text:
Percentage Distribution of Race
Instead of raw counts, perhaps we would like to display the percentage distribution of race across our data. In order to follow disclosure guidelines, any percentages must be based on rounded numerators and denominators, and then the percentage itself must be rounded as well.

race_percentages <- analysis_data |> 
  group_by(eth_recode) |>
  summarize(count=n_distinct(person_key)) |> 
  mutate(total=sum(count),
         rounded_count = if_else(as.numeric(count)<1000,
                                  round(as.numeric(count), digits=-1),
                                  round(as.numeric(count), digits=-2)),
         rounded_total = round(total, digits=-2),
         percentage = rounded_count/rounded_total*100,
         rounded_percent = round(percentage)) 

race_percentages

Calculating median wages
First, let’s calculate median wages by race. We know from our analyses above that our counts are all above 10, so we do not need to worry about checking our counts here. However, we do need to round our median wages. As stated above, wages need to be rounded to the nearest hundred.

median_wages_by_race <-  analysis_data|> 
  group_by(eth_recode,wp_enroll , .drop = TRUE) |> 
  summarize(median_wages=median(wages_q37, na.rm=T),
            round_median_wages = round(median_wages, digits=-2),
            nssn = n_distinct(person_key))

median_wages_by_race

All of these estimates look OK, since they are based on >10 SSN’s and >3 unique employers.

title: Notebook 6 - Exports
text:
Calculating Fuzzy Medians
As discussed above, exact medians cannot be exported from the ADRF. You will need to calculate fuzzy medians, by taking the average of the 45th and 55th percentiles. The ColeridgeInitiative R package contains a fuzmed function that can do this for you.

fuzzy_median <- analysis_data |> 
  summarize(fuzzy_medians=fuzmed(wages_q37))

fuzzy_median

Keep in mind, though, that we still need to round our fuzzy medians to comply with disclosure guidelines

fuzzy_median_rounded <- fuzzy_median |>
  round(digits=-2)

fuzzy_median_rounded

title: Notebook 6 - Exports
text:
Fuzzy Medians by Race
Let’s calculate fuzzy median wages by race.

fuzzy_medians_by_race <-  analysis_data|> 
  group_by(eth_recode, wp_enroll ) |> 
  summarize(real_median = median(wages_q37), 
            fuzzy_median =fuzmed(wages_q37),
            fuzzy_median_rounded=round(fuzzy_median, digits=-2),
            nssn = n_distinct(person_key))

fuzzy_medians_by_race

geom_col(position = 'dodge') +

Notebook 7 - Difference in Differences Analysis
text:
Introduction
Welcome to this lesson on Difference-in-Differences (DiD) analysis. In this notebook, we build on earlier work with descriptive comparisons and regression models to introduce a powerful analytic approach for evaluating the impact of programs and policies over time. Many of the questions we face as analysts focus on understanding whether a program changed an outcome of interest-such as wages, employment, or recidivism. DiD helps us answer these questions by comparing how outcomes evolve for participants versus similar nonparticipants, before and after a program begins.
In this lesson, we will reintroduce our familiar longitudinal perspective, where individuals are observed across multiple time periods. We will use this structure to examine changes in outcomes surrounding a specific event-such as enrolling in a workforce program-and compare those changes to a group that did not experience the event. This framework moves us beyond simple “before-and-after” comparisons by accounting for broader trends that affect everyone, such as economic shifts or seasonal patterns.
Previously, our focus was on understanding how individual characteristics were associated with outcomes at a single point in time. Difference-in-Differences extends this framework by bringing time explicitly into the analysis. Instead of asking only who has higher wages, we now ask how wages change before and after treatment, and how those changes differ between groups. By leveraging these patterns, we can better isolate the effect of the program from other factors that may influence outcomes.
By the end of this notebook, you will be able to set up and interpret a Difference-in-Differences model using program data, understand the role of comparison groups and time periods, and recognize how this method helps strengthen causal claims in applied evaluation settings. DiD provides a critical bridge between simple descriptive trends and more advanced causal inference methods, helping analysts assess program impact in a practical and transparent way.

title: Notebook 7 - Difference in Differences Analysis
text:
Explanation of Each Variable

treat - Treatment Group Indicator This variable identifies which individuals ever enrolled in the program.

If wp_enroll equals 1, the person is part of the treatment group, and treat is set to 1. If not, treat is 0.
This allows us to distinguish participants from nonparticipants across the entire dataset.

post - Post-Treatment Time Indicator This variable identifies when the treatment becomes available.

Any quarter before quarter 31 receives a 0.
Any quarter at or after quarter 31 receives a 1.
This tells the model when the program began affecting outcomes.

treat_post - Treatment ? Post Indicator This variable is the product of the two previous indicators. It equals 1 only when both conditions are true:

The person is in the treatment group (treat == 1)
The observation occurs after the treatment period begins (post == 1)
This interaction term is the key component of a DiD analysis. It represents the time period when treated individuals could actually experience program effects. The coefficient on treat_post in the DiD model estimates how much more (or less) treated individuals changed over time compared with the comparison group.
crumbs: 8  Notebook 7 - Difference in Differences Analysis

title: Notebook 7 - Difference in Differences Analysis
text:
Parts of the analysis

The formula: ui_quarterly_wages ~ treat:post

This part of the code specifies the outcome and the key explanatory variable.
ui_quarterly_wages is the quarterly wage outcome.
treat is an indicator that identifies members of the treatment group.
post is an indicator for whether the time period is after the program begins.
The interaction term treat:post equals 1 only for treated individuals in the post-treatment period.
The coefficient on treat:post is the difference-in-differences estimate. It measures how much the wage change for treated individuals differs from the wage change for the comparison group.

Fixed effects: | person_key + year_quarter_key

The bar (|) introduces fixed effects into the model.
person_key adds individual fixed effects, which control for all unobserved, time-invariant characteristics of each person (such as baseline skill or experience).
year_quarter_key adds time fixed effects, which control for quarter-specific factors that affect everyone (such as economic conditions).
These fixed effects ensure that the model compares each individual to themselves over time while accounting for any quarter-specific influences on wages.

Data and clustering

data = data_did specifies the dataset.
cluster = ~ person_key requests cluster-robust standard errors at the individual level. Because each individual contributes multiple wage observations, clustering accounts for within-person correlation in the errors and produces correct inference.







Note



What Are Cluster-Robust Standard Errors?
Cluster-robust standard errors are a way to produce more reliable statistical uncertainty estimates when the data contain repeated observations for the same individual, group, or unit. In situations like quarterly wage records, measurements from the same person are often correlated with each other rather than behaving like independent data points. Standard regression methods assume independence, which can lead to standard errors that are too small and results that appear more certain than they actually are.
Cluster-robust standard errors correct for this by allowing observations within each individual (or “cluster”) to be correlated, while still assuming different individuals are independent. This adjustment produces standard errors that more accurately reflect the structure of the data and leads to more trustworthy significance tests and confidence intervals in models such as Difference-in-Differences.




Displaying the results
summary(m_twfe) displays the estimated treatment effect and associated statistics. The most important value is the coefficient on treat:post, which represents the estimated impact of program participation on wages.

library(fixest) # need to install first

m_twfe <- feols(
  ui_quarterly_wages ~ treat:post | person_key + year_quarter_key,
  cluster ~ person_key,
  data = data_did
)

summary(m_twfe)

title: Notebook 7 - Difference in Differences Analysis
text:
Interpretation of the Difference-in-Differences Result
The Difference-in-Differences model estimated a treatment effect of 133.6, and this estimate is statistically significant. This means that, after accounting for individual characteristics and overall quarterly trends, individuals who participated in the Wagner-Peyser program experienced an average increase of 133 dollars per quarter in their wages after the program began, compared with similar individuals who did not participate.
In practical terms, this indicates that the program is associated with a meaningful improvement in quarterly earnings. The statistical significance of the estimate means that this result is unlikely to be due to random chance and provides credible evidence that program participation contributed to the observed wage gains during the post-treatment period.

title: Notebook 7 - Difference in Differences Analysis
text:
Graphical interpretation
We can generate a plot showing the basic view of the mean difference in the treatment and control groups. While this is not the same as

plot_data <- data_did %>%
  group_by(treat, year_quarter_key) %>%
  summarize(mean_wages = mean(ui_quarterly_wages, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year_quarter_key, y = mean_wages, color = factor(treat))) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 31, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Average Quarterly Wages",
    color = "Treatment Group",
    title = "Difference-in-Differences Plot of Wages Over Time"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

In this plot, we see the Wagner-Peyser enrollees had higher average wages compared to non-participants in the quarters after enrollment (quarter 31), but the difference disappears after about quarter 33, suggesting that the difference is a short-term one.
